text: The surgeon performs a posterior dissection of the pulmonary artery, revealing level XI lymph nodes along the pulmonary artery within the interlobar plane. The subcrinal space is opened, and subcrinal nodes have been dissected.

The surgeon dissects the posterior fissure early in the procedure, ensuring the space is clear of lymph nodes and undivided parenchyma. This preparation facilitates the use of a stapler later in the operation to complete the fissure from the front.

The surgeon is dissecting the fissure posteriorly, revealing the glistening surface of the pulmonary artery. A cigar sponge is placed on the first branch to the left upper lobe as it becomes visible. The posterior arm, arm four, is repositioned to provide a view of the apical hilum.

The surgeon uses a zero-degree scope to dissect the pulmonary artery from the top, ensuring clear visualization and access for later stapling from the front. The surgeon manipulates sponges while maintaining a view of the chest cavity.

The surgeon performs self-retraction to maintain exposure of the pulmonary artery plane, removing level ten and aorta pulmonary lymph nodes. This clears the lymphatic and soft tissue around the pulmonary artery, revealing its branches and anatomical details.

The surgeon continues the dissection of the pulmonary artery, exposing its branches within the incomplete fissure. The posterior and apical regions of the PA are clearly visible, allowing for strategic planning to navigate around these branches later in the procedure.

The surgeon dissects the fissure along the pulmonary artery in a case with a poor fissure. The junction of the left upper lobe and left lower lobe bronchi is identified in the hilum from an anterior approach. The inferior vein is visible, with the superior vein located cephalad. A level eleven lymph node is dissected, revealing the bronchus to the lower lobe. The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to carefully dissect around the bronchus and pulmonary artery.

The surgeon cauterizes visible bronchial arteries to prevent bleeding and continues working in the space to locate the junction between the upper and lower lobe bronchi. The pulmonary artery is exposed, and a stapler is positioned on its anterior surface. The surgeon uses a shore form stapler through the number three port to divide the thin anterior fissure, facilitating access to the space between the upper and lower lobe bronchi.

The surgeon introduces a stapler into the surgical field, aiming for the space between the upper and lower lobe bronchi. As the stapler divides the tissue, the anatomy becomes clearer, revealing the pulmonary artery. The surgeon carefully dissects around the level eleven lymph node, knowing the pulmonary artery is located behind it.

The surgeon uses a stapler to open up the space around the pulmonary artery, aiming to take approximately two to two and a half centimeters of tissue. The pulmonary artery is visible, and a sponge is used to keep the area dry. The surgeon carefully manipulates the lymph nodes to avoid fracturing them.

The surgeon places the anvil of the stapler on top of the pulmonary artery, ensuring its safe positioning. The parenchyma is divided, which opens up the space significantly. The remaining lymph node is carefully removed from the area adjacent to the pulmonary artery, allowing for a clear separation of the tissue plane.

The surgeon pulls the lung parenchyma into the stapler, working on the thick fissure to open it up. The pulmonary artery is visible, and the surgeon decides to address the lingular branch using a vascular stapler with a curved tip shore form through the anterior stapling port. The lingular branch is small, suggesting the possibility of another branch to the lingula.

The surgeon carefully removes a cigar sponge from the staple line on the pulmonary artery, ensuring no staples catch on the sponge. The posterior dissection provides a clear view of the start and end points for the next step. A stapler is positioned from the top to further open the space around the pulmonary artery.

The surgeon uses a stapler with a blue load to take a staple fire along the fissure through port number three, reducing the veil of parenchyma for better visualization. A small piece of Surgicel is visible in the surgical field. The anvil is positioned on the pulmonary artery, preparing for further dissection.

The surgeon positions the anvil on the pulmonary artery, pulls the parenchyma into the stapler, and fires the stapler to complete the final division of the fissure. A bloody sponge is exchanged for a clean one during the procedure.

The surgeon uses a second tip-up fenestrated grasper in arm four for retraction, maneuvering from front to back while staying cephalad to the superior segmental branch of the lower lobe. A remote-operated suction irrigator is used, controlled via a foot pedal at the console. The wide-open space created by prior posterior dissection allows the surgeon to insert a curved tip stapler with a blue load to complete the fissure between the upper and lower lobes. The surgeon plans to take branches of the pulmonary artery from distal to proximal using the stapler.

The surgeon encounters a small twig branch under tension and decides to use a vessel sealer to divide it, preferring this method for vessels less than half a centimeter in diameter. The retraction arm is adjusted slightly, and the surgeon continues dissecting towards the proximal pulmonary artery, taking branches until visibility becomes challenging. The previous posterior dissection facilitates easy access around these branches. An instrument exchange to a vascular load tip-up stapler from the anterior port is prepared.

The surgeon addresses a small pulmonary artery branch, considering options to either use energy or tie and cut. The surgical team proceeds anteriorly to take the vein, utilizing the posterior retraction arm for optimal exposure. The pleura is opened, revealing lymphatic tissue surrounding the vein, with the option to remove lymph nodes now or after completing the lobectomy.
The surgeon carefully identifies the top of the vein and assesses its spatial relationship to the pulmonary artery, ensuring precise manipulation to avoid mistakenly grasping the pulmonary artery.

The surgeon uses a second tip-up fenestrated grasper to navigate around the vessels, ensuring the instrument's tip is visible in the areolar tissue to avoid damaging the pulmonary artery. A vessel loop is placed, and the posterior port is prepared for stapling with a tip-up vascular load stapler.

So again, you can approach this case anterior to posterior rather than going for the fissure like I did here. In other words, you could have started just retracting the lung posteriorly, taking this vein, and then taking the PA branches and taking the fissure last. And frankly, that's how I would do a left upper lobectomy, thoracoscopically non-robotic. I find this to be a much more comfortable technique robotically, particularly if you get comfortable with that lymphadenectomy and that posterior dissection early in the case. I think that this is a way in which you're able to do really every part of the dissection under vision.

The surgeon addresses a minor injury to the chest wall, resulting in bleeding, which is quickly managed. Attention is then focused on the pulmonary artery, with the surgeon removing lymph nodes located between the bronchus and the pulmonary artery. With the posterior tissue and lymph nodes already cleared, the surgeon confidently passes the stapler from anterior to posterior along the pulmonary artery surface.
The surgeon uses a long bipolar grasper for dissection, preferring its tip and curve over other instruments like the curve dissector or Maryland dissector.

Using a zero-degree scope, the surgeon identifies the bronchus inferiorly and the pulmonary artery cephalad. The pulmonary artery is nicely dissected, revealing the crotch between the ongoing PA and its branches. The surgeon uses a tip-up grasper to push against the bronchus, ensuring the instrument tip is visible. A vessel loop is passed around the pulmonary artery branches, and a tip-up vascular stapler is used to take these branches.

The surgeon uses a curved tip vascular stapler to transect the last pulmonary artery branch, ensuring all branches are addressed by flipping the parenchyma anteriorly for a final visual check before proceeding to the bronchus.

The surgeon uses anterior-posterior compression with a stapler from port number three to take the bronchus, employing a green load. The lobectomy is completed, and the remaining lymph nodes in the aorta pulmonary window are removed before releasing the capnothorax.
